mappings.rationaleAI: The concept of God as supreme being covers the existence of the divine (A.1.a), attributes of the divine (A.1.b), the extent of human knowledge of the divine (A.1.c), and the problem of evil (A.1.d). Classical theism, pantheism, panentheism, and deism address whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a) and one or more beings (A.2.b). Monotheism (D.3.c), theism (D.3.c.i), deism (D.3.c.ii), pantheism and panentheism (D.3.c.iii), and counterarguments address atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d).

Aquinas's proofs for God's existence address the existence of the divine (A.1.a). His Summa Theologiae and faith-reason reconciliation represent theology as rational statement of religious belief (D), with doctrine and dogma (D.1.b). His natural theology is a form of theism (D.3.c.i). His synthesis of Aristotelian philosophy with Catholic doctrine exemplifies religion and science (G.2).

The written record of human religious experience from shamanism through modern new religious movements, examined cross-culturally and comparatively using evolutionary frameworks, directly constitutes the study and classification of religions (E).

Christian doctrines of incarnation, atonement, and eschatological return relate to theology's divine revelation (D.1), doctrine and dogma (D.1.b), and articles of faith (D.1.c). The resurrection and Kingdom of God teachings connect to eschatological theories (D.7) and doctrines of grace and salvation (D.8).

Muhammad's reception of divine revelations compiled as the Quran addresses the role of Sacred Scriptures in divine revelation (D.1.a). His prophetic role within strict monotheism relates to theism (D.3.c.i).

The cross-cultural typology of divine beings — monotheistic, polytheistic, henotheistic — directly addresses whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a) and one or more beings (A.2.b). Polytheism (D.3.a) and monotheism (D.3.c) are covered across traditions.
Myth as traditional narrative encoding cosmogonic, theogonic, and etiological explanations directly constitutes myth and mythology as a system of belief (F.1).

Conceptions of personal existence after death — soul immortality, resurrection, reincarnation — address the problem of immortality (A.1.d). Heaven, hell, purgatory, moksha, and nirvana connect to eschatological theories (D.7) and doctrines of salvation (D.8).

Prayer as communicative act directed toward the divine constitutes a form of religious experience (B). Its forms across traditions — liturgical, personal, contemplative — exemplify basic patterns of religious thought and action (C).

Abraham as founding patriarch who initiates the covenantal bond between God and the Jewish people directly addresses the doctrine of the Covenant (D.10). His narrative role in sacred scripture across three traditions connects to the role of Sacred Scriptures (D.1.a).

Islam's six articles of faith constitute articles of faith and religious creeds (D.1.c). Its strict monotheism represents theism (D.3.c.i). As a major world religion it is relevant to the study and classification of religions (E).

Animistic traditions with polytheistic elements relate to polytheism (D.3.a). Divination and traditional medicine connect to magic (F.2). Ancestor veneration directly addresses ancestor worship (F.6).
The moral dichotomy of good and evil directly addresses the problem of evil and suffering (A.1.d). Manichaean cosmic dualism and similar frameworks connect to religious dualism (D.3.b).
Atheism as the position that no deities exist directly addresses the question of the existence of the divine (A.1.a) and constitutes the doctrine of atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d).

Sola scriptura places Sacred Scriptures as the sole divine authority (D.1.a). The five solae represent core doctrines and dogma (D.1.b) and articles of faith (D.1.c). As a major Christian grouping it is relevant to the classification of religions (E).

The Gita as Hinduism's most influential scripture directly relates to the role of Sacred Scriptures (D.1.a). Its Vedantic non-dualism and reconciliation of Brahman with atman address pantheism and panentheism (D.3.c.iii). Karma yoga, jnana yoga, and bhakti yoga as paths to liberation connect to doctrines of salvation (D.8).

The Tao Te Ching's articulation of the impersonal Dao addresses whether the divine or sacred is personal or impersonal (A.2.a). As a foundational sacred text it relates to the role of Sacred Scriptures (D.1.a).

Descartes's transcendental arguments for God's existence directly address the existence of the divine (A.1.a). His method of radical doubt and foundationalism bear on the extent to which mankind can have knowledge of the divine (A.1.c).
Voltaire's deism and comparative religion directly address deism (D.3.c.ii). His polemics against Church dogma exemplify religion and science (G.2) and religion and society (G.3).
Philosophy's sub-field of philosophy of religion, including epistemology and metaphysics applied to religious questions, directly constitutes the philosophy of religion (A).

Religion as a comprehensive concept covering beliefs, practices, sacred texts, institutions, and worldviews across ~10,000 traditions is relevant to virtually all aspects of Section 811: philosophy of religion (A), religious experience (B), religious phenomenology (C), theology (D), classification of religions (E), other belief systems (F), and religion in relation to other human experience (G).

Christianity's doctrines of Trinity, atonement, and eschatology represent theology through divine revelation (D.1), doctrine and dogma (D.1.b), and creeds (D.1.c). Its monotheism addresses theism (D.3.c.i). As a major world religion it relates to classification of religions (E).

The Magisterium's interpretation of Scripture and Sacred Tradition relates to divine revelation (D.1) and Sacred Scriptures (D.1.a). Doctrine resting on tradition addresses doctrine and dogma (D.1.b). The Church's role in Western civilization's law and education connects to religion and society (G.3).
The Quran as God's verbatim revelation and primary source of Islamic law directly addresses theology in relation to divine revelation (D.1) and the role of Sacred Scriptures (D.1.a).

The Tao as an ineffable, impersonal underlying reality addresses whether the divine is personal or impersonal (A.2.a). Meditation practices including qigong connect to mystical theology (D.2). As a major world tradition it relates to the classification of religions (E).

Metaphysics as the study of the fundamental structure of reality, including ontology and cosmology, underpins philosophical inquiry into the existence of the divine (A.1.a) and basic questions about the nature of ultimate reality (A.1).
Ontology's investigation of what entities exist and the categories of reality bears directly on the question of the existence of the divine or sacred (A.1.a).

Epistemology's study of the nature, sources, and limits of knowledge, including religious knowledge as a specialized branch, directly addresses the extent to which mankind can have knowledge of the divine or sacred (A.1.c).

The tension between reason and faith as a major philosophical concern addresses the extent of human knowledge of the divine (A.1.c). Rational inquiry applied to religious claims connects to religion and science (G.2).
Subjective experience, self-awareness, and phenomenology of consciousness provide the cognitive framework within which religious experience occurs (B).

Kant's critique of pure reason established limits on metaphysical knowledge of God (A.1.c), while his philosophy of religion grounds morality rather than metaphysics in the divine, addressing basic questions about the existence and nature of the divine (A.1, A.1.a).

Enlightenment Deism (D.3.c.ii) and secularism address alternatives to traditional theism, while empiricist challenges to religion connect to atheism and agnosticism (D.3.d). The movement's emphasis on reason over revelation exemplifies religion and science (G.2) and religion and society (G.3).
Secularism as the principle that public institutions should operate independently of religious authority directly addresses religion and society (G.3).

Confucianism as an ethical-social tradition raises questions about whether it constitutes a religion, relevant to the classification of religions (E). Its emphasis on social relationships and governance connects to religion and society (G.3) and religion and morality (G.4).
